🧠 Top 5 Defense Mechanisms for the NCLEX
Defense mechanisms are unconscious ways in which clients protect themselves from stress and emotional pain. NCLEX questions often test whether you can recognize the behavior and identify the correct coping pattern.

Here are 5 high-yield

🩺 Chronic Kidney Disease Stages for NCLEX
CKD stages are a must-know for the NCLEX. Questions often test how GFR levels connect to disease severity and complications.

CKD stages based on GFR
• Stage 1: GFR 90 or higher, normal function, but kidney damage present
• Stage 2: GFR

recent_posts.caption:
ECG/EKG interpretation study guide & workbook by Nurse Sarah. One of the biggest mistakes nursing students make when learning ECG interpretation is that they try to memorize what a rhythm looks like instead of understanding what makes each rhythm unique.

In this study guide, you

Floor Nursing Slang for New Nurses:
-Pushing meds = giving meds rapidly IV
-Vein rolled = vein shifted during needle stick 
-IV blew = vein failed; need to stick again 
-Hard stick = no good veins for IV/blood draw 
-Hemolyzed = blood sample unusable, need to redraw 
-Prime the l

Nurse Anatomy Quiz:

What is the largest vein in the human body?
a. superior vena cava
b. inferior vena cava
c. femoral vein
d. pulmonary vein

Answer is B: Inferior vena cava. This is the body's largest vein. It takes deoxygenated blood from the lower body to the right atrium. T

Vitamin deficiency symptoms to remember for NCLEX, nursing school, and while working as a nurse:

↓ Vit A = night blindness, poor immunity
↓ Vit D = bone pain/fractures, RICKETS
↓ Vit K = bleeding/bruising, Warfarin ANTIDOTE
↓ Vit C = bleeding gums, poor wound healing, SCURVY (re

ECG Cardiac NCLEX Review Quiz for nursing school students & new grads! 
A patient is currently taking spironolactone. 

You notice the following ECG changes on the patient's ECG: tall, peaked T waves; wide QRS complex; and ST segment depression. The patient's morning labs come ba

Starting your first IV doesn't always go as you'd planned in your head 😂

#nurse #nurselife #nursing #nursingschool

Nurse ECG/EKG quiz:

Tall, peaked t waves are seen with which electrolyte imbalance:

A. hypercalcemia
B. hyperkalemia
C. hypocalcemia
D. hypokalemia

Answer is B: Hyperkalemia. This means there’s too much potassium in the blood (normal ~3.5–5 mEq/L). Think “hyper = high,” so you

NCLEX Prep tip: Know when to cardiovert vs. defibrillate!

-CARDIOVERT: Pulse PRESENT. Used for UNSTABLE a-fib, svt, a-flutter, v-tach w/ pulse. Goal: CONVERT rhythm. 

SYNCHRONIZED shock w/ R-wave. Sedate if possible.

-DEFIBRILLATE: Pulse GONE. Used for v-fib & pulseless v-tach

ECG interpretation - Top rhythms 
Nurse Sarah's ECG/EKG cheat sheet for nursing school students, nurses, and more.

Normal sinus rhythm = everything is working as it should
Sinus tachycardia = pumping too fast but regular
Sinus bradycardia = pumping too slow but regular

Not all pills are meant to be split—here’s how to know what’s safe, what’s not… and when in doubt, ask the pharmacist! 💊⁠
⁠
#medpasstips #nurselife #pharmacology #medicationsafety #safemedadmin

- Types of Heart Block -⁠
⁠
🫀Heart blocks occur when electrical signals from the atria to the ventricles are delayed or blocked⁠
⁠
🫀This leads to changes in the relationship between P waves and QRS complexes⁠
⁠
#nursingschool #heartblock #EKG #cardiacnursing #NCLEX⁠

Crystalloids vs. Colloids ⁠
⁠
💧Crystalloids⁠
SMALL molecules ⁠
Hypertonic, isotonic, and hypotonic solutions are examples of crystalloids⁠
These are LESS expensive⁠
Used for immediate fluid replacement⁠
LOWER risk for allergic reactions⁠
⁠
🩸Colloids⁠
LARGE molecules⁠
Blood prod

Trach Emergencies 🚨🚑🫁⁠
⁠
–What is a tracheostomy (trach)? –⁠
🫁A tracheostomy is a tube placed in the trachea to help a patient breathe⁠
⁠
–What is a trach emergency? –⁠
